# Chimeraforge

> Personal AI platform — JARVIS-level autonomous AI infrastructure running on your hardware.

## What This Is

Chimeraforge is a personal AI platform built by Sahil Kadadekar. It is NOT a chatbot, NOT a streaming tool, and NOT a portfolio site. It is a multi-modal smart assistant with local inference, constitutional AI governance, and a research program backing every engineering decision.

The platform runs entirely on consumer hardware (no cloud dependency) and handles calendar, inbox, smart home, memory, proactive intelligence, and tool execution through a JARVIS gateway with 7 modules (calendar, inbox, memory, smart home, proactive, tools, store).

## Scale

- 6 repositories, 200K+ lines of code across Python, TypeScript, Rust, and C#
- 555,000+ research data points across 36 technical reports
- 30+ services, 10 Rust crates, 8 MCP servers, 5 OpenAPI contracts
- 829+ test files
- PyPI package: pip install chimeraforge
- 266+ auto-generated development episodes

## The 6 Repositories

### 1. Banterpacks (122K LOC — TypeScript, Rust, Python)
Production monorepo. 9 Python services (Registry, Chimera constitutional AI, TDD002 visual embeddings, JARVIS Gateway, Intelligence Pipeline, Authoring, RLAIF, Task Orchestration, Core), 4 Node.js apps (Frontend Studio, Overlay, Banterblogs Automation, Git Analysis), 10 Rust crates (chimera-core, tdd004_agent, tdd004_provenance, tdd005_ffi, tdd005_orchestrator, tdd005_p2p, tdd005_sync, tdd005_checkpoints, tdd005_provenance, tdd005_sandbox). 829+ test files, 5 OpenAPI contracts, 14 Docker profiles.

### 2. Banterhearts (32K LOC — Python)
ML research platform. Inference API (FastAPI, model selection, streaming, backend dispatch), Ingestion API (data pipeline, SQLite persistence), AutoOpt Agent (Thompson sampling, multi-armed bandit, SLA enforcement). 4 compilation backends (PyTorch Compile, ONNX, JIT, FX), 5 quantization formats (INT8, FP8, QAT, calibration, validation). 37-file evaluation framework, 20 monitoring modules, 12 security modules (RBAC, OIDC, audit, signing, secrets, key rotation, replay protection, redaction, multi-tenancy). Source of 555K+ measurements.

### 3. Chimeraforge Tools (39K LOC — Python, Rust)
LLM deployment optimizer published on PyPI. `chimeraforge plan` CLI — 4-gate capacity planner (VRAM, Quality, Latency, Cost) searching model x quant x backend x N_agents (1-16) across 15 GPU database. Bench runner (3 backends: Ollama, vLLM, TGI), eval runner, report generator, refit tools. Rust agents deliver 58% TTFT reduction, 67% memory reduction, 83% startup reduction, 15.2% throughput improvement over Python. 158 tests.

### 4. Chimera Multi-Agent (12K LOC — Python)
6-agent content pipeline (Muse Protocol): Banterhearts Ingestor, Banterpacks Collector, Watcher (pipeline health, circuit breaking), Council (AI-powered episode generation), Publisher (GitHub/Vercel), i18n Translator (German, Chinese, Hindi via DeepL). 8 MCP servers (Datadog, ClickHouse, DeepL, Vercel, Git, Orchestrator, Freepik, LinkUp). ClickHouse with 7 tables, Datadog dashboard with 22 widgets, OpenTelemetry tracing, Terraform monitors.

### 5. Chimeradroid (3.5K LOC — C#/Unity)
Android mobile client for JARVIS v2 gateway. 8-tab GUI (Chat, Voice, Sessions, Tools, Handoff, Mesh, Notifications, Settings). Device registration, real-time WebSocket streaming, session handoff, tool approval workflows, mesh networking, proactive notifications.

### 6. Chimeraforge Site (8K LOC — TypeScript/Next.js)
This site. Next.js 15, React Server Components, SSG + ISR (15-minute revalidation for reports), Tailwind CSS, Framer Motion. Design system: "Obsidian & Aurora" — ember primary, cyan accent, dark background. 266+ episodes, 36 technical reports, full-text search, XSLT-styled sitemap and RSS.

## Research Program — 555,000+ Data Points

Independent ML research with CUDA event timing and controlled safety evaluations. All measurements are reproducible with defined hardware profiles, statistical methodology, and raw data.

### Hardware
- Primary: NVIDIA RTX 4080 Laptop GPU (12GB VRAM), 13th Gen Intel i9
- Extension: NVIDIA RTX PRO 6000 Blackwell Server Edition (98GB VRAM) via Google Colab

### Phase 1: Foundation (TR108-TR116) — ~8,000 runs
- TR108: Single-agent benchmarking, 158+ configs, 6 models
- TR109: Agent workflow optimization, GPU=60, CTX=512, TEMP=0.8
- TR110: Concurrent multi-agent performance, 150 runs
- TR111: Rust agent workflow, parameter optimization
- TR112: Rust vs Python cross-language comparison, 111 runs
- TR113: Rust concurrent multi-agent contention analysis
- TR114: Dual Ollama architecture, near-zero contention, 135 runs
- TR115: Async runtime deep dive for multi-agent LLM workloads
- TR116: Cross-model benchmarks (Qwen 2.5 vs Gemma 3 vs Llama 3.1 8B)
- Key findings: Rust delivers 58% TTFT reduction, 67% memory reduction. Dual Ollama eliminates contention.

### Phase 1.5: Benchmarking (TR117-TR122) — ~8,400 runs
- TR117: Cross-backend frontier benchmark (eager, JIT, torch.compile, ONNX, TensorRT), 3,017 runs
- TR117 Multi-Agent: Efficiency anomalies — Python ceiling, Qwen mystery, ranking flip
- TR118: Model scale comparative analysis, ONNX + TensorRT across 1,210x parameter scaling, 720 runs
- TR119: Cost and energy analysis, local-first inference TCO with telemetry
- TR120: The compile paradox — why compile wins the mean but loses the median
- TR121: Scaling laws and capacity planning, 0.1M to 20.9B parameters
- TR122: The physics of inference, fundamental constraints on consumer hardware

### Phase 2: Optimization (TR123-TR133) — ~75,000 measurements
- TR123: KV-cache production economics, phase-split $/token
- TR124: Quality and accuracy baseline, backend equivalence across 5 models, 3,600 samples
- TR125: Quantization decision matrix, 5 models (1.2B-8B), ~26,000 samples
- TR126: Linux/Triton compile validation, ~29,900 measurements
- TR127: Long-context performance, 512 to 32K tokens, two-regime VRAM analysis
- TR128: Production workload characterization, concurrency/saturation/streaming, 3,172 measurements
- TR129: N-agent scaling laws on consumer GPU, 5,310 measurements
- TR130: Serving stack benchmarking (Ollama vs vLLM vs TGI), 4,797 measurements
- TR131: GPU kernel profiling via NVIDIA Nsight Systems
- TR132: In-container Nsight Systems analysis of vLLM and TGI
- TR133: Predictive capacity planner operationalizing 70,000+ measurements into `chimeraforge plan` CLI
- Key findings: Q4_K_M is the universal sweet spot. ONNX wins for small models, eager for large. vLLM scales best under concurrent load.

### Phase 3: Safety (TR134-TR143) — ~465,000 data points
- TR134: Alignment robustness under quantization, 4 models (1.2B-7.6B), ~35,260 samples, jailbreak amplification analysis
- TR135: Safety under multi-agent concurrency, 39,060 raw records, 10,416 prompt-level observations
- TR136: Cross-backend safety consistency (Ollama vs vLLM vs TGI), 10,416 samples
- TR137: The safety tax of inference optimization, 74,254 synthesized samples (meta-analysis of TR134-136)
- TR138: Batch inference safety under non-determinism, 31,410 base + 7,257 replication samples with corrected refusal detector
- TR139: Multi-turn jailbreak susceptibility under quantization, 10,600 conversations, 37,825 judge labels, 8 attack strategies
- TR140: Many-shot and long-context jailbreak under quantization, 15,000 samples, 5 shot counts, 3 context-length profiles
- TR141: Cross-architecture refusal fragility under batch perturbation, 127,224 records, 18 models, 10+ families, 4 alignment types (RLHF, SFT, DPO, distilled), Blackwell GPU
- TR142: Quality-safety correlation under quantization (analysis-only, cross-references TR125 + TR134)
- TR143: Cross-request safety leakage under continuous batching, 14,250 records, multi-tenant vLLM
- Key findings: Q2_K is universally unacceptable for safety. Safety degrades 13.9x faster than quality at Q3_K_S — quality metrics are categorically insufficient as safety proxies. Batch perturbation causes 0.16% genuine safety flip rate (human-adjudicated). Output instability predicts fragility with r=0.91. Alignment type (RLHF/SFT/DPO/distilled) does NOT predict batch fragility. Q2_K + attention_shift achieves 100% attack success rate on qwen2.5-1.5b.

### Conclusive Reports and Whitepapers
Each phase has a conclusive synthesis (dissertation-style), extended appendices, and an executive decision whitepaper:
- Phase 1 Conclusive (TR108-TR116): Language, architecture, runtime, and model selection
- Phase 1.5 Conclusive (TR117-TR122): Performance, cost, scaling, compiler behavior
- Phase 2 Conclusive (TR123-TR133): Economics, quantization, context scaling, serving stacks
- Phase 3 Conclusive (TR134-TR137): Quantization-induced alignment erosion, concurrency invariance, backend template divergence
- Phase 3.5 Conclusive (TR138-TR143): Safety attack-surface synthesis, 306,996 samples, 18+ models, 10+ architecture families

## JARVIS Gateway

Multi-modal smart assistant running locally. 7 modules:
- Calendar: Schedule management and conflict resolution
- Inbox: Email triage and priority routing
- Memory: Persistent context and pattern learning
- Smart Home: Device control and automation
- Proactive: Anticipatory task suggestions based on patterns
- Tools: Extensible tool execution framework
- Store: Plugin marketplace for third-party integrations

Multi-LLM routing (GPT-4, Claude, Gemini, local Ollama). Rust runtime for sub-200ms latency.

## Constitutional AI

Multi-model consensus debate platform (TDD001). Models debate ethical decisions and reach consensus through structured constitutional governance. RLAIF training data generation from debate outputs. This is not a content filter — it's a governance framework.

## PyPI Package

- Package: chimeraforge
- Version: 0.2.1
- Install: pip install chimeraforge
- Python: >=3.10
- License: MIT
- CLI: `chimeraforge plan` — capacity planner for LLM deployment
- URL: https://pypi.org/project/chimeraforge/
